research_series.series_name: Nutritional Deficiency Markers, Fatigue, Mood, Cognition, Dizziness, and Recovery

patterns.pattern_name: Iron deficiency with or without anemia is associated with fatigue, mood symptoms, sleep disturbance, and attention/executive deficits

mediator.name: iron_biomarkers_and_oxygen_delivery
mediator.pathway: iron-dependent oxygen delivery, neurotransmitter synthesis, mitochondrial metabolism, and brain electrophysiology
mediator.mechanism: low iron availability may impair oxygen transport and neural/metabolic processes; symptoms can occur before anemia

inference_rule.plain_language_rule: Low iron availability, including non-anemic iron deficiency, increases likelihood of fatigue, mood/sleep complaints, and attention/executive deficits; correction is most supported when true baseline deficiency exists.

hypotheses.reason: Cross-sectional, cohort, RCT, and biomarker evidence consistently link iron deficiency to fatigue; RCT evidence supports improvement after correction in deficient individuals.
Iron biomarkers are linked to attention/cognition in adolescents and adults; some intervention evidence supports biomarker-to-brain-function-to-cognition pathways.

evidence.summary: Iron deficiency, even without anemia, is strongly associated with fatigue, neuropsychiatric complaints, attention/memory/executive deficits, and sleep problems. Supplementation benefits are supported primarily when baseline deficiency is present.

key_sources.title: Evaluation of a Single Dose of Ferric Carboxymaltose in Fatigued, Iron-Deficient Women – PREFER
key_sources.relevant_finding: Correction of iron deficiency improved fatigue/cognition in iron-deficient participants.
key_sources.limitations: Benefit should not be generalized to non-deficient individuals.

key_sources.title: Beyond anemia: a comprehensive analysis of iron deficiency symptoms in women and their correlation with biomarkers
key_sources.relevant_finding: Symptoms can occur without anemia; transferrin saturation may be a sensitive marker.

limitations: Supplementation benefit depends on baseline deficiency

patterns.pattern_name: Functional vitamin B12 deficiency predicts cognitive and neuropsychiatric impairment more reliably than serum B12 alone

mediator.name: MMA_homocysteine_supported_B12_deficiency
mediator.pathway: one-carbon metabolism, myelin maintenance, and neurological function
mediator.mechanism: B12 deficiency can impair methylation and neurological function; functional markers outperform serum B12 alone

inference_rule.plain_language_rule: B12-related cognitive/mood inference should rely on functional deficiency evidence such as elevated MMA or homocysteine, not serum B12 alone.

hypotheses.reason: Functional B12 deficiency markers are linked to memory/executive dysfunction and cognitive decline risk.

evidence.summary: B12 deficiency supported by MMA/homocysteine predicts memory/executive/processing-speed impairment and neuropsychiatric symptoms; supplementation helps truly deficient individuals but not necessarily low-normal serum B12 without functional deficit.

key_sources.title: Low Vitamin B12 Levels: An Underestimated Cause Of Minimal Cognitive Impairment And Dementia
key_sources.relevant_finding: Low B12 linked to MCI/dementia outcomes.

key_sources.title: Clinical manifestations and outcomes of chronic nitrous oxide misuse: A systematic review
key_sources.relevant_finding: Functional B12 disruption can occur even with misleading serum values.

patterns.pattern_name: Vitamin D, zinc, magnesium, omega-3, and folate show weaker or context-dependent links to mood, sleep, frailty, and cognition

mediator.name: micronutrient_biomarkers
mediator.pathway: immune, neurotransmitter, neuromuscular, and membrane-related pathways
mediator.mechanism: associations may reflect true deficiency, inflammation, diet quality, sunlight exposure, frailty, or comorbid disease

inference_rule.plain_language_rule: Low vitamin D, zinc, folate, magnesium, and omega-3 may raise probability of mood/sleep/frailty or cognitive problems, but causality is context-dependent and supplementation should not be inferred as helpful without confirmed deficiency.

hypotheses.reason: Systematic review/meta-analysis evidence supports associations for vitamin D and zinc, but intervention evidence is less definitive.

evidence.summary: Vitamin D and zinc are associated with depression/postpartum depression/cognitive frailty/sleep disturbance; magnesium evidence is limited by biomarker problems; omega-3 status is linked to MCI/frailty but direct energy/brain fog evidence is limited.

key_sources.title: Current Evidence on the Association of Micronutrient Malnutrition with Mild Cognitive Impairment, Frailty, and Cognitive Frailty among Older Adults
key_sources.relevant_finding: Low B vitamins, vitamin D, zinc, and omega-3 linked to MCI/frailty/cognitive frailty.

key_sources.title: Invisible Links: Associations Between Micronutrient Deficiencies and Postpartum Depression
key_sources.relevant_finding: Vitamin D/zinc and other deficiencies linked to postpartum depression; magnesium biomarker limitations noted.

limitations: supplementation evidence weaker than association evidence
serum magnesium may not reflect intracellular status
omega-3 direct brain fog/energy evidence limited

negative_or_uncertain_findings.claim: Supplementation improves fatigue/mood/cognition without baseline deficiency.
negative_or_uncertain_findings.reason: RCTs/meta-analyses generally show no clear benefit unless true biochemical deficiency is present.

negative_or_uncertain_findings.claim: Ferritin alone always identifies iron deficiency symptom burden.
negative_or_uncertain_findings.reason: Ferritin is an acute-phase reactant and may be misleading with elevated CRP/inflammation; transferrin saturation can be more sensitive in some contexts.

negative_or_uncertain_findings.claim: Serum magnesium reliably captures magnesium-related mood/cognition risk.
negative_or_uncertain_findings.reason: Serum magnesium may not reflect intracellular magnesium status.

high_value_candidate_nodes.definition: Low iron storage marker; interpretation confounded by inflammation.
Marker of reduced circulating iron availability; may detect early symptomatic iron deficiency.
B12 deficiency supported by MMA and/or homocysteine rather than serum B12 alone.
Supplementation when baseline deficiency is absent.

research_gaps: Whether correcting non-anemic iron deficiency improves objective cognition across all age groups.
Whether correcting subclinical B12/folate functional deficits improves executive function.
How combined micronutrient deficiencies interact to affect fatigue, mood, and cognition.

most_supported_patterns: Iron deficiency with or without anemia is linked to fatigue, mood/sleep complaints, and attention/cognition.
Functional B12 deficiency is linked to cognitive and neuropsychiatric impairment.
patterns_requiring_caution: serum magnesium as a standalone marker
recommended_next_queries: How do sex hormones and menstrual cycle phase influence mood, energy, cognition, sleep, HRV, and pain?
How do gut-brain axis and GI symptoms influence mood, fatigue, and cognition?
summary.overall_assessment: Micronutrient branch is useful for BodyState Mapper, but must remain marker-specific. Iron and functional B12 are strong; vitamin D/zinc/folate/magnesium/omega-3 are more context-dependent. Supplementation should not be inferred as helpful without demonstrated baseline deficiency.

works_cited.title: Evaluation of a Single Dose of Ferric Carboxymaltose in Fatigued, Iron-Deficient Women – PREFER
Beyond anemia: a comprehensive analysis of iron deficiency symptoms in women and their correlation with biomarkers
Low Vitamin B12 Levels: An Underestimated Cause Of Minimal Cognitive Impairment And Dementia
Current Evidence on the Association of Micronutrient Malnutrition with Mild Cognitive Impairment, Frailty, and Cognitive Frailty among Older Adults
